annotations.text: Dysarthria, nasal speech, diplopia, and vertigo all suggest brain-stem dysfunction.

The pattern matches the description by Wilkinson and Russell in their classic review of giant-cell arteritis.
Although this elderly, hypertensive man probably did have some atheromatous disease, the vessels involved and the laboratory evidence suggest that he had giant-cell arteritis.
One possible explanation for multiple brain infarctions is multiple emboli from the heart.

Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the presence of ANCA is the best test for systemic vasculitis.

A rate of 47 mm per hour or more is a finding that is 92 percent sensitive for giant-cell arteritis, and an elevated level of C-reactive protein (a markedly elevated level in this case, at 29.8 mg per liter) is 100 percent sensitive.
